question.text: A financial planner notices that client Gopal always invests in the same 3 stocks his father invested in, despite poor recent performance. Which behavioral bias is Gopal exhibiting?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Gopal exhibits familiarity bias (investing only in stocks he knows through family tradition) combined with anchoring (holding onto these stocks regardless of performance, anchored to his father's choices). **The Trap:** This is not herd mentality (Gopal is not following the crowd) or recency bias (he is ignoring recent poor performance). It is a combination of familiarity and anchoring biases. **Cross-Exam Context:** Real-world situations often involve multiple overlapping biases. The exam may test your ability to identify the dominant bias or combination of biases. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Gently challenge such inherited portfolio biases with performance data. Show Gopal how a diversified portfolio would have performed compared to his concentrated 3-stock approach.
